stepAnswer: One way is to use the unit circle directly, recognizing $$\frac{5\pi}{4}$$ is in Quadrant III. Another way is to use a sum or difference identity, such as cos((pi + (pi)/(4))) or cos(((3pi)/(2)) $$-$$ (pi)/(4)).
stepTitle: Which statement accurately describes how to evaluate $$cos\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right)$$ using two different trigonometric identities or concepts?
answerLatex: One way is to use the unit circle directly, recognizing $$\frac{5\pi}{4}$$ is in Quadrant III. Another way is to use a sum or difference identity, such as cos((pi + (pi)/(4))) or cos(((3pi)/(2)) - (pi)/(4)).
choices: One way is to use the unit circle directly, recognizing $$\frac{5\pi}{4}$$ is in Quadrant III. Another way is to use a sum or difference identity, such as cos((pi + (pi)/(4))) or cos(((3pi)/(2)) $$-$$ (pi)/(4)).
One way is to use the Pythagorean identity $${sin}^2$$ $$x$$ + $${cos}^2$$ $$x$$ $$=$$ $$1$$. Another way is to use the double angle identity cos(2x) $$=$$ $$2{cos}^2$$ $$x$$ $$-$$ $$1$$.
One way is to use the $$half-angle$$ identity $$cos\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)$$ $$=$$ +/-sqrt((1+cos x)/(2)). Another way is to use the reciprocal identity cos $$x$$ $$=$$ (1)/(sec x).
One way is to use the cofunction identity cos $$x$$ $$=$$ sin(((pi)/(2)) $$-$$ x). Another way is to use a calculator to find the decimal value.
